blocks.content: The five of them walk out together as the evening comes in.

The carnival lights are fully on now — the strings of them along the midway burning warm against the deepening blue of the sky, the rides lit in their particular carnival way: excessive and cheerful and somehow right. The crowd has thinned toward the gate. The evening has that quality of things arriving at their close — not ending, just becoming the next thing.
Nathan and Lena are debating something about the penguin's long-term future. Maya is walking a half-step apart in the way she walks when she is thinking, which is almost always.
Ethan and Cassandra are at the back of the group.

She is not thinking about the future right now. Not because she has stopped being afraid of it — she will always be afraid of it, that is simply the shape of her life — but because the future is the future and this is now. And she has spent three years, since sixteen, learning the most important thing she will ever learn:
Now is not a consolation prize.
